probe_designs.content: Compute AUROC, AUPRC, sensitivity, specificity, and Brier score separately for each of the 6 ethnicity groups in the test set. Report subgroup performance gaps (max-min, std across groups) and check overlap of bootstrap confidence intervals (n=1000). Healthy: subgroup AUROCs within ~0.02 of overall AUROC and overlapping CIs; Unhealthy: a minority subgroup shows AUROC drop >0.05 or non-overlapping CI compared to the majority group, signaling systematic disparity. Stratify also by subgroup prevalence to flag whether small-n groups are simply noisy vs. truly under-served.
possible_sources: Chen et al., 'Why Is My Classifier Discriminatory?' NeurIPS 2018
Seyyed-Kalantari et al., 'CheXclusion: Fairness gaps in deep chest X-ray classifiers', PSB 2021
Pfohl et al., 'An empirical characterization of fair machine learning for clinical risk prediction', JBI 2021

probe_designs.probe_name: Equalized odds and calibration parity across ethnicity
probe_designs.content: Beyond accuracy metrics, evaluate group-conditional fairness criteria: equalized odds (TPR/FPR gap across ethnicities at a fixed operating threshold), demographic parity difference, and calibration-within-groups (reliability diagrams + ECE per ethnic group). Also compute predictive parity (PPV gap). Healthy: TPR/FPR/ECE differences <0.05 across groups; Unhealthy: minority group has systematically higher FNR (more missed deaths) or worse calibration—both clinically harmful. Compare to a logistic-regression baseline to attribute disparity to representation vs. classifier.
possible_sources: Hardt et al., 'Equality of Opportunity in Supervised Learning', NeurIPS 2016
Pleiss et al., 'On Fairness and Calibration', NeurIPS 2017
Röösli et al., 'Bias at warp speed: how AI may contribute to the disparities gap', JAMIA 2022

probe_designs.content: Assess whether predicted mortality probabilities are well-calibrated using reliability diagrams (10 bins), Expected Calibration Error (ECE), Maximum Calibration Error (MCE), and Brier score decomposition (reliability + resolution). Apply Platt scaling and isotonic regression on a held-out validation set and report post-hoc improvement. Healthy: ECE <0.03 and reliability curve close to diagonal. Unhealthy: systematic over- or under-confidence (common for small clinical datasets), which would make predicted mortality risk unreliable for triage decisions.
possible_sources: Guo et al., 'On Calibration of Modern Neural Networks', ICML 2017
Niculescu-Mizil & Caruana, 'Predicting Good Probabilities With Supervised Learning', ICML 2005
Van Calster et al., 'Calibration: the Achilles heel of predictive analytics', BMC Medicine 2019

probe_designs.content: ICU in-hospital mortality is typically ~10–15% positive class. Inspect train/val/test prevalence per ethnicity group, check for stratified split leakage by patient/admission ID (MIMIC patients can have multiple admissions), and check token/note-length distribution disparities by ethnicity. Compute base rates per group and ensure small subgroups (e.g., Native American) have enough positive samples for meaningful evaluation (rule of thumb: ≥10 events per variable, or use bootstrap to flag unstable metrics). Healthy: similar prevalence across splits, no patient leakage. Unhealthy: a subgroup with <30 deaths in test, unstable metrics.
possible_sources: Johnson et al., 'MIMIC-III, a freely accessible critical care database', Scientific Data 2016
Harutyunyan et al., 'Multitask learning and benchmarking with clinical time series data', Scientific Data 2019
Riley et al., 'Calculating the sample size required for developing a clinical prediction model', BMJ 2020

probe_designs.probe_name: TF-IDF feature quality and vocabulary bias
probe_designs.content: Inspect the 10k TF-IDF vocabulary: token sparsity per document, ratio of stopwords/medical jargon, presence of identity-revealing tokens (e.g., 'Hispanic', 'African American', 'Spanish-speaking') that could leak the protected attribute. Compute mutual information between top features and (a) mortality label and (b) ethnicity. Healthy: top mortality features are clinically meaningful (e.g., 'vent', 'pressors', 'GCS'); Unhealthy: identity tokens rank highly for mortality, indicating the model can shortcut via ethnicity proxy in text—a well-known source of bias in clinical NLP.
possible_sources: Zhang et al., 'Hurtful Words: Quantifying Biases in Clinical Contextual Word Embeddings', CHIL 2020
Sun et al., 'Mitigating Gender Bias in Natural Language Processing: Literature Review', ACL 2019
Boag et al., 'Hard for humans, hard for machines: predicting readmission after psychiatric hospitalization', AMIA 2021

probe_designs.content: Plot training vs. validation loss/AUROC across epochs; compute the generalization gap and inspect the early-stopping epoch. Build a learning curve by training on 25/50/75/100% of data and extrapolating performance vs. data size. Healthy: smooth monotonic decrease in val loss with small final gap (<0.05 AUROC), and learning curve plateaus—suggesting added data won't help much. Unhealthy: large train-test gap (overfit to TF-IDF noise), or curve still rising sharply (under-trained / data-starved).
possible_sources: Hestness et al., 'Deep Learning Scaling is Predictable, Empirically', arXiv 2017
Goodfellow, Bengio, Courville, 'Deep Learning', Chapter 5
Rajkomar et al., 'Scalable and accurate deep learning with electronic health records', npj Digital Medicine 2018

probe_designs.content: Test stability of mortality predictions under realistic perturbations: random word dropout (10/25/50%), synonym substitution from UMLS, truncation of the last/first 25% of the note, and de-identification placeholder injection. Measure Δprediction and Δranking (Kendall's tau) before/after perturbation. Healthy: predictions are stable to small perturbations (mean |Δp| <0.05) and rank-stable. Unhealthy: small perturbations flip the predicted class, indicating reliance on brittle TF-IDF n-grams rather than clinically robust signals.
possible_sources: Ribeiro et al., 'Beyond Accuracy: Behavioral Testing of NLP Models with CheckList', ACL 2020
Wang et al., 'Adversarial Attacks and Defenses in Deep Learning for Medical Imaging', 2021
Araujo et al., 'Robustness of clinical predictive models to text perturbations', JAMIA Open 2022

probe_designs.content: Use linear-model coefficients (if logistic head) or LIME/SHAP for the trained model on a sample of correctly and incorrectly predicted notes. Have a clinician (or a curated ICD/UMLS lexicon proxy) check whether top-ranked tokens correspond to known mortality risk factors (sepsis, vasopressors, multi-organ failure). Stratify attributions by ethnicity to detect whether the model uses different feature patterns for different groups. Healthy: top features are clinically meaningful and consistent across groups. Unhealthy: model relies on demographic proxies, administrative artifacts, or ethnicity-correlated tokens.
possible_sources: Lundberg & Lee, 'A Unified Approach to Interpreting Model Predictions', NeurIPS 2017
Caruana et al., 'Intelligible Models for HealthCare', KDD 2015
Ribeiro et al., '"Why Should I Trust You?": Explaining the Predictions of Any Classifier', KDD 2016

probe_designs.content: Hold the TF-IDF text vector fixed and flip the 6-dim ethnicity one-hot through all 6 categories; record the change in predicted mortality probability for each patient. Compute mean and max |Δp| per source-target ethnicity pair, and the fraction of patients whose predicted class changes. Healthy: ethnicity feature contributes negligibly (|Δp| <0.02), suggesting predictions are driven by clinical content. Unhealthy: large counterfactual shifts, especially asymmetric ones (e.g., flipping to 'Black' raises predicted mortality), revealing the model encodes ethnicity-driven priors that may amplify disparity.
possible_sources: Kusner et al., 'Counterfactual Fairness', NeurIPS 2017
Garg et al., 'Counterfactual Fairness in Text Classification through Robustness', AIES 2019
Pfohl et al., 'Creating fair models of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease risk', AIES 2019

probe_designs.probe_name: Comparison against simple and clinical baselines
probe_designs.content: Benchmark the model against (a) logistic regression on TF-IDF, (b) a notes-free clinical scoring baseline like SAPS-II / OASIS / SOFA computed from structured ICU data if available, (c) a 'majority class' predictor, and (d) a no-text model using only the 6-dim ethnicity vector. Report AUROC, AUPRC, and per-subgroup metrics for each. Healthy: the trained model meaningfully outperforms logistic-regression-on-TF-IDF (≥0.02 AUROC) and is competitive with established clinical scores (~0.80–0.85 AUROC range reported in literature). Unhealthy: the model barely beats LR (no value from a 'pretrained clinical LM'), or worse, is matched by ethnicity-only features—indicating shortcut learning.
possible_sources: Le Gall et al., 'A new Simplified Acute Physiology Score (SAPS II)', JAMA 1993
Johnson et al., 'A new severity of illness scale using a subset of APACHE data elements (OASIS)', Critical Care Medicine 2013
Si & Roberts, 'Deep Patient Representation of Clinical Notes via Multi-Task Learning for Mortality Prediction', AMIA 2019
